name: Visual Reasoning

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q page 105 Q112 (mirror-image figure question — flagged by TNPSC's own answer key as disputed/under expert-committee review)
careers360.com / reasoningability.com - mirror image and water image rules
2025_q page 112 Q120 (figure-relationship analogy — 4 pairs of figures, find the pair that breaks the pattern, GST2AM/2025 exam, with answer key, image-verified)
2025_q page 121 Q132 (embedded-figure question — swastika-symbol figure, GST2AM/2025 exam, with answer key, image-verified)
learnfrenzy.com - mirror image of clock rule and worked examples

researchNotes: The one direct real hit is unusually flagged: the source page itself carries a printed notice that 'THIS QUESTION AND ANSWER ARE TO BE PLACED BEFORE THE EXPERT COMMITTEE FOR FINAL DECISION' — meaning even TNPSC wasn't fully confident in its own answer key for that specific mirror-image question. Included as evidence that mirror-image figure questions ARE a real tested format, but the specific marked answer is treated as unconfirmed rather than authoritative. The core technique (mirror vs water image rules) comes from general reasoning-prep sources since this is a well-established, stable concept not specific to any one year's paper. Expanded 2026-07 pass: found two more real, image-confirmed hits in the OCR question bank — a figure-relationship-analogy question (Q120: four (i)-(ii) figure pairs, find which pair does NOT share the 'same shape, just resized/rotated' relationship the others do) and an embedded-figure question (Q132: a swastika-like symbol, find which candidate shape is hidden inside it). Both involve genuine printed diagrams that can't be reliably reproduced as literal images in this app's text-only format, so Q120 was reframed as a faithful WORDED description of the actual figures (verified against the source page image) rather than an image, and Q132 is cited descriptively in reading only, without forcing a specific image-dependent mock question. To round out the syllabus's non-verbal-reasoning formats without needing images, this pass adds mirror/water image of clock times (pure arithmetic, no image needed), mirror/water image facts about digits (0/1/8 unchanged, 6↔9 swap trap), mirror image of whole words built from vertically-symmetric letters, a classic paper-folding-and-cutting puzzle (solved by pure fold-symmetry logic), and shape-classification-by-side-count questions (converting named polygons to their side-count turns an unfamiliar 'shape analogy' into a familiar number analogy) — all genuine, well-established non-verbal-reasoning archetypes, tagged web-research/authored accordingly.

reading.intro: Visual Reasoning is 1 of 6 named Reasoning skills, testing image-based logic rather than text/number puzzles — mirror images, water images, and embedded figures are the standard formats. Note: TNPSC's own answer key has at least one acknowledged disputed mirror-image question, a useful reminder that on visual questions specifically, double-check your own reasoning rather than assuming the 'obvious' answer is automatically right.

qa.question: What is the rule for a MIRROR image, and what stays the same vs. flips?
qa.answer: A mirror image flips LEFT and RIGHT (the right side of the object appears on the left, and vice versa), while TOP and BOTTOM stay the same. Imagine holding the figure up to a vertical mirror.

qa.question: What is the rule for a WATER image, and how is it different from a mirror image?
qa.answer: A water image flips TOP and BOTTOM (it appears upside-down, as if reflected in still water below the object), while LEFT and RIGHT stay the same. This is the opposite axis of flipping compared to a mirror image.

qa.question: What is the best strategy for solving mirror/water image questions quickly?
qa.answer: Focus on the most unique or asymmetric part of the figure first (not the symmetric parts, which look the same either way) — mentally flip just that distinctive part along the correct axis (vertical line for mirror, horizontal line for water), and use it to eliminate options before checking the rest.

qa.question: What is an 'embedded figure' question, and what does it test?
qa.answer: A question where a simple shape is hidden within a more complex, cluttered figure, and you must identify which of the answer-choice shapes appears embedded within it. Tests visual scanning and pattern-matching rather than flipping/rotation.

qa.question: Real exam (flagged as disputed by TNPSC itself): A figure is shown, and you must choose its correct mirror image from 4 options. What's the most reliable way to verify your choice, given that even the official answer key has had disputed cases here?
qa.answer: Don't just eyeball it — explicitly trace which side each distinctive feature should land on after a left-right flip, point by point, rather than relying on a quick visual impression. Given that TNPSC's own committee has had to review at least one mirror-image answer, treat your worked-through reasoning as more trustworthy than a snap visual judgment, and double-check rather than rushing.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q page 112 Q120): Four pairs of figures (i)-(ii) are shown. Pair 1: a crosshatched arrow-cross shape paired with a smaller identical copy. Pair 2: a house-shaped pictogram paired with a completely different arrow/flag-shaped figure. Pair 3: a bold hourglass/bowtie shape paired with a smaller, rotated copy of the same shape. Pair 4: a five-pointed star outline paired with a smaller copy of the same star. Which pair does NOT follow the relationship the other three share?
qa.answer: Pair 2. In pairs 1, 3, and 4, figure (ii) is simply a resized or rotated version of the SAME shape as figure (i). In pair 2, figure (ii) is an entirely different, unrelated shape from figure (i) — not a resized copy of the house pictogram at all. That inconsistency is what the real exam marked as the correct answer.
qa.tip: For 'figure relationship' analogy questions, check first whether figure (ii) is truly the SAME shape as figure (i) (just scaled, rotated, or re-shaded) — the pair where (ii) is a genuinely different shape is the odd one, no matter how visually busy or plain the other options look.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q page 121 Q132): A swastika-like symbol built from interlocking right-angle strokes is shown as figure (X), and you must pick which of 4 candidate shapes is embedded/hidden within it. What's the general strategy for this format?
qa.answer: Embedded-figure questions require tracing the actual printed line segments of the complex figure to see if they can be regrouped into one of the simpler candidate shapes, without adding or removing any lines. The real exam's confirmed answer for this specific swastika figure was a square containing a diamond formed by its own diagonals and midlines — a reminder that the 'hidden' shape is often a simple geometric primitive built entirely from lines already present in the busier figure, not something exotic or unrelated.
qa.tip: Trace along the actual printed lines of figure (X) with your finger or pencil-tip rather than trying to 'see' the embedded shape at a glance — these figures are deliberately camouflaged by surrounding clutter.

qa.question: Method — mirror image of a clock: A clock shows 4:20. What does its mirror image show?
qa.answer: Treat 12:00 as the reference and subtract the shown time from it: 12:00 − 4:20 = 7:40. So the mirror image shows 7:40.
qa.tip: Formula: mirror time = 12:00 − actual time (borrow as 11:60 if the minutes don't subtract cleanly). This works because a vertically-mirrored clock face reads the same distance from 12 on the opposite side.

qa.question: Which digits look completely unchanged in BOTH their mirror image and their water image, and which two digits swap with each other in a water/upside-down image?
qa.answer: 0, 1, and 8 are symmetric enough to look unchanged either way. 6 and 9 are the classic swap-trap: turn a 6 upside down (water image) and it looks exactly like a 9, and vice versa — a very frequently tested detail.
qa.tip: Memorize '0-1-8 unchanged, 6-and-9 swap' as a fixed fact — it resolves most digit-based mirror/water questions instantly without needing to visualize anything.

qa.question: Method — mirror image of a whole word: What is the mirror image of the word 'HOT', given that H, O, and T are all vertically-symmetric letters (each looks the same when individually flipped left-right)?
qa.answer: Mirroring a word does two things at once: it reverses the LEFT-TO-RIGHT ORDER of the letters, and it mirrors each individual letter. Since H, O, and T all look identical when mirrored on their own, only the order changes — so the mirror image reads 'TOH'.
qa.tip: Separate the two effects: first reverse the letter order, then check each letter's own shape. Words built entirely from vertically-symmetric letters (A, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y) only have their ORDER reversed in a mirror image — the letters themselves don't distort, which makes them the easiest words to test with.

qa.question: What is a 'paper folding and cutting' question, and what's the key to solving it without seeing the final unfolded result drawn out?
qa.answer: A square or rectangular sheet is folded one or more times, a shape is cut from the folded corner/edge, and you must determine what the pattern of holes looks like once fully unfolded. The key is to count how many LAYERS of paper the cut passes through (which equals the number of times the paper was folded, doubled each fold) and to track exactly which point of the ORIGINAL, unfolded sheet the cut corner corresponds to — each layer becomes one mirrored hole, positioned symmetrically around whatever point/line the folds were made along.
qa.tip: Number of resulting holes = 2^(number of folds), as long as every fold passes through the cut. A single fold (1 crease) → 2 holes; two folds (perpendicular creases) → 4 holes, arranged symmetrically across both fold lines.

qa.question: What is a 'figure series' or 'figure completion' question, and what should you track across the sequence?
qa.answer: A sequence of figures is shown where a consistent visual rule changes step-to-step — e.g. a shape rotates by a fixed angle each time, an element's count increases, or shading alternates. Track ONE property at a time (rotation angle, number of sides/dots, position of a marked element) across the whole sequence rather than absorbing the whole figure at once, then apply the same rule to predict the missing/next figure.
qa.tip: If a figure series seems to change in several ways simultaneously, isolate and track just ONE visual property per pass through the sequence (first rotation, then count, then shading) — trying to track everything at once is the most common way to misread these questions.

qa.question: In a shape-classification/analogy question that uses only named polygons and no numbers or letters at all (e.g. 'triangle is to square as square is to ?'), what trick turns it into a familiar pattern?
qa.answer: Convert each named shape to its number of sides (triangle=3, square=4, pentagon=5, hexagon=6, and so on) and look for an arithmetic relationship between the numbers, exactly as with a number analogy. 'Triangle : square :: square : ?' becomes '3 : 4 :: 4 : ?', revealing a '+1' relationship — so the answer is a pentagon (5 sides).
qa.tip: Whenever a visual reasoning question uses named polygons instead of pictures, silently substitute each shape's side-count — it turns an unfamiliar 'shape analogy' into a number analogy you already know how to solve.

questions.prompt: In a MIRROR image of a figure, which pair of sides swap?
questions.explanation: Left and right swap; top and bottom stay the same.
questions.trick: Mirror = vertical axis = left-right swap. Pair this fact with 'water = horizontal axis = top-bottom swap' and you'll never confuse the two.

questions.prompt: In a WATER image of a figure, which pair of sides swap?
questions.explanation: Top and bottom swap; left and right stay the same.
questions.trick: Water = reflection in still water BELOW the object, so naturally it's the top-bottom axis that flips, not left-right.

questions.prompt: The letter 'b' would appear as which letter in its mirror image (left-right flip)?
questions.explanation: 'b' has its stem on the left with the bowl attached at bottom-right. Flipping left-right moves the stem to the right with the bowl at bottom-left — that's the letter 'd'.
questions.trick: For asymmetric letters, track just the ONE distinctive feature (here, which side the stem is on) rather than the whole letter shape.

questions.prompt: The letter 'b' would appear as which letter in its water image (top-bottom flip)?
questions.explanation: Flipping 'b' top-to-bottom keeps the stem on the left but moves the bowl from bottom-right to top-right — that's the letter 'p'. (Mirror and water images of the same letter generally differ — here mirror gives 'd', water gives 'p'.)
questions.trick: b/d/p/q are the classic 4-way letter-flip family: b→d (mirror), b→p (water), b→q (rotate 180°) — memorize this single family and you can derive any of the four instantly.

options: Visual scanning and pattern-matching within a cluttered figure
questions.explanation: Visual scanning/pattern-matching to find a hidden shape within a complex figure.
questions.trick: Trace the actual printed lines of the complex figure rather than relying on a snap visual impression — embedded shapes are deliberately camouflaged.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q Q120): Four figure-pairs (i)-(ii) are shown. Pairs 1, 3, and 4 each show figure (ii) as a resized/rotated copy of the SAME shape as figure (i). Pair 2 shows a house pictogram (i) paired with a completely different arrow/flag shape (ii). Which pair breaks the pattern the other three share?
questions.explanation: In pair 2, figure (ii) is an unrelated shape, not a resized/rotated copy of figure (i) — unlike the other three pairs.
questions.trick: Check first whether (ii) is truly the same shape as (i), just scaled or rotated — the pair where it's a genuinely different shape is always the odd one out.

questions.prompt: A clock shows 3:15. What does its mirror image show?
questions.trick: Mirror time = 12:00 minus the shown time (borrow as 11:60 when minutes don't subtract cleanly).

questions.prompt: A clock's mirror image shows 7:10. What is the actual time on the clock?
questions.explanation: 12:00 − 7:10 = 4:50. (The mirror formula is symmetric — applying it a second time recovers the original time.)
questions.trick: The same 12:00-minus-time formula works in reverse: apply it to the mirror time shown to recover the real time.

questions.prompt: Which digit looks completely unchanged in its water image (upside-down reflection)?
questions.explanation: 8 has both horizontal and vertical symmetry, so it looks identical upside down. 6 and 9 actually turn into each other upside down.
questions.trick: Memorize '0-1-8 unchanged, 6-and-9 swap' — this single fact answers almost every digit mirror/water question.

questions.prompt: In a water image, the digit '6' appears as which digit?
questions.explanation: Flipping 6 upside down (top-bottom) turns it into 9 — the classic 6/9 water-image swap.
questions.trick: 6 and 9 are the one pair of digits that transform INTO each other in a water image — don't confuse this with '0/1/8 unchanged'.

questions.prompt: What is the mirror image of the word 'HOT' (H, O, and T are all vertically-symmetric letters)?
questions.explanation: Mirroring reverses the left-to-right letter order; since H, O, T each look the same when individually mirrored, only the order flips, giving TOH.
questions.trick: Reverse the letter order first, then check each letter's own mirrored shape — symmetric letters (H, I, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y, A, M) only have their order flipped.

questions.prompt: A square sheet of paper is folded in half, then in half again (perpendicular to the first fold), so all folds meet at one corner of the resulting small square. A small triangle is cut from that corner. When fully unfolded, how many triangular holes appear, and how are they arranged?
options: 4 holes, symmetric around the sheet's center
questions.explanation: 2 folds create 4 paper layers at that corner (2² = 4). The corner where both fold-creases meet corresponds to the CENTER of the original sheet, so the cut reproduces symmetrically in all 4 quadrants around the center when unfolded.
questions.trick: Number of holes = 2^(number of folds) whenever every fold passes through the cut point. Locate which point of the ORIGINAL sheet the folded corner corresponds to — that point becomes the center of symmetry for all the resulting holes.

questions.prompt: Triangle is to square as square is to which shape (using number of sides as the hidden rule)?
questions.explanation: Triangle(3):Square(4) is a '+1 side' relationship. Applying the same '+1' to square(4) gives a pentagon(5).
questions.trick: Convert named shapes to their side-count and solve it exactly like a number analogy (3:4::4:?).

questions.prompt: Three figure-pairs follow a 'the enclosed shape has exactly one side fewer than the enclosing shape' pattern: triangle-in-square, square-in-pentagon, and pentagon-in-hexagon. Which pair BREAKS this pattern?
questions.explanation: Triangle(3)-in-square(4), square(4)-in-pentagon(5), and pentagon(5)-in-hexagon(6) all follow 'inner shape has exactly 1 fewer side'. Hexagon(6)-inside-square(4) reverses this — the LARGER-sided shape is enclosed within the smaller one.
questions.trick: Convert every named shape to its side-count first; the option that breaks the ARITHMETIC relationship (not just the one that 'looks' different) is the odd one out.

questions.prompt: The most reliable way to solve an embedded-figure question is to:
options: Guess based on overall visual impression
Trace the actual printed lines of the complex figure to see which simple shape they form
Count the total number of lines in each answer option
Always pick the most symmetric-looking option
questions.explanation: Embedded shapes are camouflaged by surrounding clutter — tracing the real printed lines (without adding or removing any) reveals which candidate shape is genuinely hidden inside.
questions.trick: Never trust a snap visual impression on embedded-figure questions — physically trace the lines.

questions.prompt: A clock shows 10:35. What does its mirror image show?
questions.trick: Mirror time = 12:00 minus the shown time; borrow as 11:60 whenever the minutes alone won't subtract.

questions.prompt: A figure's water image is obtained by flipping it across:
questions.explanation: Water image = reflection across a horizontal axis, so top and bottom swap while left and right stay the same.
questions.trick: Mirror = vertical axis (left-right swap). Water = horizontal axis (top-bottom swap). Keep the axis name and the swap it causes paired together in memory.
